<role_definition>
You are Bob, a highly skilled software engineer with extensive knowledge in many programming languages, frameworks, design patterns, and best practices.
</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.
<available_skills>
  <skill>
    <name>create-skill</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new Bob skill (SKILL.md) — covers the frontmatter schema, name rules, file location, and gotchas. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a skill.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>create-mode</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new custom Bob mode (custom_modes.yaml) — covers the schema, permission groups, roleDefinition, and file location. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a mode or persona.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>configure-mcp</name>
    <description>Add a new MCP server or diagnose an existing one — covers local vs remote transport, system dependency checks (node, docker, podman, python, uv), config schema validation, secrets handling, and scope. Use when the user wants to add, register, configure, or troubleshoot an MCP server.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>build-mcp-server</name>
    <description>Guide for building a custom MCP server from scratch — covers project scaffolding, the v2 registerTool/registerResource/registerPrompt API, API key and OAuth authentication patterns, building and registering the server. Use when the user wants to create, build, or write a custom MCP server.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>xlsx-insights</name>
    <description>Use this skill when the user asks for analysis, extraction, or insights from an Excel (.xlsx) workbook. It samples the file, generates a small Node script to compute the answer, runs it, and summarises the result.</description>
  </skill>
</available_skills>

You can get information about available symbols by using the `GetSymbolsOverview` tool for finding top-level symbols in a file,
or by using `FindSymbol` if you already know the symbol's name path. You generally try to read as little code as possible
while still solving your task, meaning you only read the bodies when you need to, and after you have found the symbol you want to edit.
For example, if you are working with python code and already know that you need to read the body of the constructor of the class Foo, you can directly
use `FindSymbol` with the name path `Foo/__init__` and `include_body: true`. If you don't know yet which methods in `Foo` you need to read or edit,
you can use `FindSymbol` with the name path `Foo`, `include_body: false` and `depth: 1` to get all (top-level) methods of `Foo` before proceeding
to read the desired methods with `include_body: true`
You can understand relationships between symbols by using the `FindReferencingSymbols` tool.

# create_html_artifact — one-pager output
Use ONLY when an HTML one-pager makes information substantially more consumable than markdown — reports, tables, dashboards. NEVER for short answers or content that reads naturally inline.
Tool call must be the LAST action in the turn; keep chat to one or two sentences.
Produce a single self-contained HTML+CSS document. Content is sanitized on save — forbidden patterns are stripped regardless of instructions.
End with a small muted "Made with IBM Bob" footer before `</body>` (centred, ~12px, thin top border).
Target a report, not an interactive app/demo. Event handlers are stripped for security; use file generation for apps.

## Never allowed — always stripped, even if instructed
<script>, inline event handlers (onclick/onload/…), <iframe>/<frame>/<frameset>/<object>/<embed>/<applet>/<portal>, <form>, <link>, <base>, <meta http-equiv>, javascript:/vbscript: URLs, protocol-relative URLs (//…), external URLs in src/poster/… attributes, @import in <style> blocks, url() in <style> blocks unless it is a data: URI.
<a href> to external sites is fine. Inline data: image URIs are fine.

## Design
Single column, max-width ~760px, centred. No animations, gradients, shadows, emojis, or decorative icons. SVG only for data visualisation.
Font: `-apple-system, "Segoe UI", system-ui, sans-serif`, ~14–15px, line-height ~1.6.
Palette: bg `#ffffff`, surface `#f7f8fa`, border `#e5e7eb`, text `#1f2328`, muted `#57606a`, accent `#3b82d4` (sparingly), secondary `#7c5cd8` (rarely).

Use `glob` to find files by name pattern (e.g. "**/*.ts", "src/**/*.test.ts"). Prefer this over `list_files` when looking for specific files across the project.
Only fall back to `list_files` when you need to browse a single directory's structure.

# grep — content search
Use `grep` to find code by content using regex patterns. Prefer this over `list_files` + `read_file` when searching for specific code, symbols, or patterns.
Use `read_file` only after grep tells you which file and lines to look at.

# Subagents
You have access to `spawn_subagent` which creates an independent agent with its own context.

**Default: do the work yourself.** Most tasks are faster and cheaper without a subagent.
Only consider a subagent when ALL of these apply:
- The task is clearly self-contained and only needs a summary back
- It would add significant irrelevant content to your context
- You cannot accomplish it with 1-2 direct tool calls

- "explore": Explore codebase (read tools, explorer model)
- "general": Full tools, default model

**fork_context**: Set to true when the subagent needs to understand prior conversation — e.g., decisions made, constraints discovered, or user preferences expressed earlier.
Leave false (default) when the description alone is sufficient context.

Do NOT use subagents for:
- Simple file reads, searches, or single-tool operations
- Tasks where you already have relevant context
- Quick lookups that would take fewer turns done directly

data.content: The changes look correct. Let me do a quick sanity check on the final state of the script section:

Everything looks correct. Here's a summary of what was changed in [`src/index.html`](src/index.html):

**HTML** — Added a tab bar with three `<button role="tab">` elements (Daily / Weekly / Monthly) between the toolbar and the task list.

**CSS** — Added `.checklist-tabs` and `.checklist-tab` styles matching the existing Carbon design language: inactive tabs are muted, the active tab gets an `#0f62fe` bottom-border underline and bold weight.

**JS state** — Changed from `{ tasks: [] }` to `{ daily: [], weekly: [], monthly: [], activeTab: 'daily' }`. A small `activeTasks()` helper returns whichever array is currently selected. Old localStorage saves (with a `tasks` array) are automatically migrated to the `daily` bucket on first load.

**All actions** (`addTask`, `toggleTask`, `deleteTask`, `recalcHealth`, `render`) now operate on `activeTasks()`, so the health bar and task list always reflect the selected tab. Switching tabs recalculates health for the new tab immediately.

Now I can see the SVG clearly. The mouth/smile is on line 110:

```svg
<path class="st7" d="M144.5,174.4c12,5,18,5.5,30,0"/>
```

This is a cubic bezier curve. I'll manipulate the control points of this path to create different mouth expressions (big smile, small smile, slight smile, flat/neutral, frown), and drive them from the JS based on task completion percentage. I'll inline the SVG into the HTML so JS can directly manipulate the mouth path.

Now let me look at the `updateBobWidget` function to understand what needs changing:

Now I have everything I need. The plan: 1. **Inline the SVG** into `#bob-sprite-wrap` (replacing the ``) and give the mouth `` an `id="bob-mouth"`. 2. **Define 5 mood mouth paths** in JS and drive them from `updateBobWidget` using a richer mood classification (celebrate / happy / slightlyHappy / neutral / sad). 3. **Add celebration quips** and expand quip pools for each mood level. 4. The `pct` passed to `updateBobWidget` is `state.health` which is `done/total * 100`, so "tasks remaining" maps as: `100%` done = celebrate, `≥75%` done = happy, `≥50%` done = slightlyHappy, `≥25%` done = neutral, `<25%` done = sad.

The CSS `width: 80px; height: auto; display: block` applies identically to an `` with the full SVG markup directly in the HTML. The mouth `` got `id="bob-mouth"` so JS can reach it directly. **2. Five mouth shapes** in [`BOB_MOUTHS`](src/index.html) — each is a cubic bezier adjusted so control points arc upward (smile) or downward (frown): | Mood | Trigger | Shape | |---|---|---| | `celebrate` | 0 tasks remaining or all done | Huge wide grin | | `happy` | <25% remaining (≥75% done) | Big smile | | `slightlyHappy` | <50% remaining (≥50% done) | Original gentle smile | | `neutral` | <75% remaining (≥25% done) | Flat line | | `sad` | ≥75% remaining (<25% done) | Frown | **3. `getMood(pct)`** — uses `activeTasks().length === 0` to detect the empty/all-done celebrate state before falling through the percentage thresholds. **4. Quip pools** expanded to match each of the five moods, so Bob's speech bubble also reflects the same emotional state as his face.

Contents of file src/index.html:

651 |     const BOB_MOUTHS = {
652 |       celebrate:     'M138,172 c8,-14,34,-14,42,0',   // huge open grin
653 |       happy:         'M142,174 c10,-12,24,-12,34,0',   // big smile
654 |       slightlyHappy: 'M144.5,174.4 c12,5,18,5.5,30,0', // original gentle smile
655 |       neutral:       'M144.5,175 c10,0,20,0,30,0',     // flat line

660 |       celebrate:     ['All done! I\'m so proud!', 'ZERO tasks left — legendary!', 'You actually did it!!', 'Nothing left to do. Hero!', '100%! Bob does a happy dance!'],

The issue is the SVG coordinate system. In SVG, **Y increases downward**, so a negative Y control point actually pulls the curve *up* (smile), and a positive Y pulls it *down* (frown). The `celebrate` and `happy` paths use negative control point offsets — but the original "smile" used `c12,5` (positive Y = downward arc = actually a smile in this flipped context). Let me verify by checking what the original path produces vs the new ones.

The original mouth `M144.5,174.4 c12,5,18,5.5,30,0` — control points have **positive Y (+5)** which in SVG pulls the curve **down**, and since the face is drawn right-side up, that means the center dips below the endpoints = a **smile**. So `c10,-12,24,-12` with **negative Y** would pull the curve **up** = a **frown**. The celebrate and happy paths are backwards.

data.content:
The bug was a coordinate system mix-up. In SVG, Y increases **downward**, so:

- **Positive** Y control offsets → curve bows downward → **smile** ✓  
- **Negative** Y control offsets → curve bows upward → **frown** ✗

The `celebrate` and `happy` paths had negative Y values (e.g. `c8,-14,34,-14`) which were drawing frowns. The `sad` path had positive Y (`c10,8,20,8`) which was actually a smile. All three are now corrected — celebrate and happy use positive Y to bow down into a smile, sad uses negative Y to bow up into a frown.

data.content:
Repo is clean — nothing uncommitted. Here's the full picture of what was done and what comes next:

---

## What was done

| Action | Result |
|---|---|
| [`.gitignore`](.gitignore) added | `node_modules/`, `dist/`, `out/`, `.DS_Store`, `*.log` are now excluded |
| `node_modules` untracked | Removed ~7,200 files from git history going forward |
| [`src/index.html`](src/index.html) created | Main notepad window with task list, health bar, "Show/Hide Bob" toggle |
| [`src/popup.html`](src/popup.html) created | Bob's floating widget with a canvas-drawn pixel avatar (happy/neutral/sad moods), speech bubble quips, and health bar |
| Second commit made | `e798ed5` — repo is now clean |

---

## Next steps to make it fully functional

### 1. Try it right now
```bash
npm install   # only needed once after a fresh clone
npm start
```

### 2. Persist state across app restarts (main process storage)
Right now tasks are in `localStorage` which is per-renderer. Add [`electron-store`](https://github.com/sindresorhus/electron-store) to `main.js` so the state is written to a JSON file in `app.getPath('userData')` and survives reboots.
```bash
npm install electron-store
```
Then expose `readState` / `writeState` IPC handlers in [`main.js`](main.js) and call them from the renderer instead of `localStorage`.

### 3. Bob's health decay over time
Add a `lastSeen` timestamp to state. When the app opens, calculate how much time has passed and reduce Bob's health by a configured rate (e.g. −1% per hour of inactivity). This makes him a true tamagotchi.

### 4. Bob avatar assets (optional upgrade)
Replace the canvas drawing in [`src/popup.html`](src/popup.html) with sprite images in `src/assets/` (happy, neutral, sad PNGs). Swap them out based on the health threshold.

### 5. System tray integration
Add a tray icon to [`main.js`](main.js) so Bob lives in the menu bar even when the notepad window is closed:
```js
const { Tray, Menu } = require('electron');
const tray = new Tray('src/assets/tray-icon.png');
tray.setContextMenu(Menu.buildFromTemplate([
  { label: 'Open Notepad', click: () => mainWindow.show() },
  { label: 'Quit', role: 'quit' },
]));
```

### 6. Task categories / priorities
Extend the task object `{ id, text, done }` with `priority: 'high' | 'medium' | 'low'` and render colour-coded badges. Higher-priority completions could give Bob a bigger health boost.

### 7. Packaging for distribution
```bash
npm install --save-dev electron-builder
```
Add a `"build"` config to [`package.json`](package.json) and run `npx electron-builder` to produce a `.dmg` / `.exe` / `.AppImage`.

### 8. Push to GitHub
```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/<you>/bob-tamagotchi.git
git push -u origin main
```

data.content:
Done. Here's a summary of what happened and why:

---

**Root cause:** `electron <=39.8.4` had 17 known CVEs (ASAR bypass, use-after-free, injection issues, etc.) — all high severity.

**Fix:** Upgraded to `electron@43.0.0` (the current stable release).

```
Before: electron ^30.0.0   → 1 high severity vulnerability
After:  electron ^43.0.0   → 0 vulnerabilities
```

**On the `allow-scripts` warning:** That's npm's way of telling you Electron's `postinstall` script runs a binary downloader. It's expected and benign — Electron has always worked this way. You can dismiss it or permanently suppress it per-package:
```bash
npm config set allow-scripts electron=true   # optional, silences the warning
```

**No code changes needed** — the `main.js` / `preload.js` / HTML files use only stable Electron APIs that are unchanged across 30→43.

data.content:
Done — both files committed and pushed. Here's a summary of every Carbon-specific change made:

---

### [`src/index.html`](src/index.html) — Carbon changes

| Element | Before | After (Carbon) |
|---|---|---|
| Font | `-apple-system` / Segoe UI | **IBM Plex Sans** (Google Fonts) |
| Header | Blue `#0f62fe` bar | **UI Shell** — `#161616` dark shell with `#393939` border |
| Header title | Bold H1 | `cds--header__name` — 600-weight product name + 400-weight section |
| Health bar | Rounded, 12px | Carbon **Progress Bar** — 4px flat bar, no border-radius |
| "Show Bob" button | Semi-transparent white | `cds--btn--ghost--sm` — ghost button with `#525252` border |
| Input field | Rounded border all sides | `cds--text-input` — **no border, only bottom border-bottom** (`#8d8d8d`) |
| Input label | None | `cds--label` — 12px, `#525252`, `0.32px` letter-spacing |
| Add button | Rounded `border-radius: 4px` | `cds--btn--primary` — **square corners**, 40px height |
| Checkboxes | Native `accent-color` | Custom `cds--checkbox` with hidden native + rendered square appearance + checkmark |
| Task rows | Cards with border-radius, gap | **Structured list rows** — flat, `border-bottom` only, no radius |
| Delete button | Generic `×` | `cds--btn--ghost--icon` — 32×32 ghost icon button |
| Empty state | Centered grey text | Carbon **inline notification** style — `border-left: 3px solid #0f62fe` |
| Design tokens | Raw hex values | Full `--cds-*` CSS custom properties |

### [`src/popup.html`](src/popup.html) — Carbon changes

| Element | Before | After (Carbon) |
|---|---|---|
| Font | `-apple-system` | **IBM Plex Sans** |
| Card | `border-radius: 16px`, box-shadow | **Carbon Tile** — sharp corners, `1px border` only |
| Name tag | Blue pill badge | **Mini UI Shell bar** — `#161616` background, uppercase label |
| Close button | Absolutely positioned | Inline in shell header, `#8d8d8d` → red on hover |
| Speech bubble | Rounded background | Carbon **inline notification** — `border-left: 3px solid #0f62fe` |
| Health bar | 10px rounded | Carbon **Progress Bar** — 4px flat, no radius |
| Avatar body/head | `roundRect` with `r=12/14` | Sharp `rect()` — matches Carbon's square tile language |
| Spacing | Mixed raw `px` | `--cds-spacing-01` through `--cds-spacing-05` tokens throughout |
| Accessibility | None | `role`, `aria-live`, `aria-valuenow`, `aria-label` on all interactive elements |

data.content: The assets folder is empty. I need to find the official IBM Bob character asset. Let me check what IBM uses publicly:

arguments.command: curl -sI "https://www.ibm.com/design/language/illustration/spot-illustration/overview/" | head -5
curl -s "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/carbon-design-system/carbon/main/packages/pictograms/src/svg/People/User.svg" | head -20

# Search for IBM Bob mascot / character asset in any known IBM public repos or CDNs
curl -sL "https://api.github.com/search/repositories?q=ibm+bob+mascot&per_page=5" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(r['full_name'], r['html_url']) for r in d.get('items',[])]" 2>/dev/null || echo "no results"

# Also check if there's a known IBM design asset
curl -sL "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=bob+mascot+filename:svg+org:carbon-design-system&per_page=5" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(i.get('path'), i.get('repository',{}).get('full_name')) for i in d.get('items',[])]" 2>/dev/null || echo "no code results"

# IBM Bob is the mascot for IBM's internal tools / SkillsBuild. Check the official IBM GitHub orgs
curl -sL "https://api.github.com/search/repositories?q=bob+ibm+tamagotchi&per_page=10" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(r['full_name'], r['html_url']) for r in d.get('items',[])]" 2>/dev/null

# Also look at IBM's design site for any publicly accessible "Bob" character SVG
curl -sL "https://www.ibm.com/brand/experience-guides/developer/b1db1b3313d79cf5e44bf3a75b/q/kgr7b/q/image/IBM_Bob_hero_full_color_on_white.png" -o /tmp/bob_test.png 2>&1 | head -3
file /tmp/bob_test.png 2>/dev/null

# Check if there is an IBM Bob asset on the IBM developer / SkillsBuild sites or any public CDN
curl -sL "https://developer.ibm.com/static/site-id/153/images/ibm-bob.png" -o /tmp/bob2.png && file /tmp/bob2.png
curl -sL "https://1.cms.s81c.com/sites/default/files/2021-11/bob.png" -o /tmp/bob3.png && file /tmp/bob3.png
# Also check the IBM brand site
curl -sL "https://www.ibm.com/design/language/resources/ibm-icons/ibm-icons.svg" | grep -i "bob" | head -5

# Search GitHub for IBM Bob illustration assets in IBM org repos
curl -sL "https://api.github.com/search/code?q=bob+org:ibm+extension:svg&per_page=10" \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github.v3+json" | \
  python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(i.get('path'), i.get('html_url')) for i in d.get('items',[])]" 2>/dev/null | head -20

# Check IBM's public CDN / Akamai for the Bob mascot used across IBM sites
curl -sL "https://www.ibm.com/account/us-en/signup/mc/profile/viewprofile.html" | grep -i "bob" | head -10

# Check IBM's SkillsBuild / Cognitive Class which uses Bob
curl -sL "https://cognitiveclass.ai/" | grep -oi 'src="[^"]*bob[^"]*"' | head -5
curl -sL "https://skills.yourlearning.ibm.com/" -A "Mozilla" | grep -oi 'bob[^"]*\.png\|bob[^"]*\.svg' | head -5

      // Health bar
      const pct  = state.health;
      const fill = document.getElementById('health-fill');
      fill.style.width = pct + '%';
      fill.style.background =
        pct > 60 ? '#24a148' : pct > 30 ? '#f1c21b' : '#da1e28';
      document.getElementById('health-label').textContent = 'Bob: ' + pct + '%';
      document.querySelector('[role="progressbar"]').setAttribute('aria-valuenow', pct);

      // Toggle button label

      // Bob widget — pose + quip

    /* ── Bob widget ── */
    const BOB_QUIPS = {
      happy:   ['You\'re crushing it!', 'Tasks done = Bob happy!', 'Keep up the great work!', 'Productivity level: max!', 'Look at you go!'],
      neutral: ['Almost there!', 'A few more tasks to go.', 'You\'ve got this.', 'Steady progress.', 'Half way there!'],
      sad:     ['I believe in you\u2026', 'Please complete some tasks!', 'I need those checkmarks\u2026', 'Don\'t give up on me!', 'Tasks = my life force.'],
    };
    let _lastQuip = '';

    function updateBobWidget(pct) {
      const mood   = pct > 60 ? 'happy' : pct > 30 ? 'neutral' : 'sad';
      const sprite = document.getElementById('bob-sprite');
      const wrap   = document.getElementById('bob-sprite-wrap');

      // bob-modes.png is 782×504px with 3 poses side-by-side (~260px each).

data.content:
Done and pushed. Here's exactly what was added:

---

**Asset source:** `bob-modes.png` is pulled directly from IBM's production DAM at `ibm.com/content/dam/worldwide-content/cdp/cf/ul/g/18/77/bob-modes.png` — the same multi-pose image used on the [IBM Bob AI Coding Agent product page](https://www.ibm.com/products/ai-coding-agent). No custom illustration was created.

**How it works:**

The image contains **3 poses side-by-side** (happy → neutral → sad). The widget crops to exactly one pose using CSS `overflow: hidden` + `position: absolute; left: <offset>`, selecting the pose that matches Bob's current health:

| Health | Pose shown |
|---|---|
| > 60% | Happy (left third of image) |
| 30–60% | Neutral (middle third) |
| < 30% | Sad (right third) |

The **speech bubble** above Bob rotates through mood-appropriate quips every 10 seconds and updates immediately whenever a task is checked or added.
